mission.title: Install and Configure MicroK8s
mission.description: MicroK8s is a lightweight, fully conformant Kubernetes distribution designed for developers, IoT, and edge computing. It is ideal for quick setups and local development environments.

steps.title: Install MicroK8s
steps.description:
Install MicroK8s using Snap. This command installs the latest stable version of MicroK8s. For specific versions, use the `--channel` flag.
```bash
sudo snap install microk8s --classic --channel=1.31/stable
```

steps.title: Verify Installation
steps.description:
Check that MicroK8s is installed and running correctly. Use the following commands to verify the status of the cluster and nodes:
```bash
sudo microk8s status --wait-ready
sudo microk8s kubectl get nodes
```

steps.title: Configure kubectl Access
steps.description:
To use the standard kubectl command, configure your kubeconfig file:
```bash
sudo microk8s kubectl config view --raw > $HOME/.kube/config
```

steps.title: Enable Add-ons
steps.description:
Enable essential add-ons such as DNS and the Kubernetes dashboard:
```bash
sudo microk8s enable dns
sudo microk8s enable dashboard
```

steps.title: Post-Install Configuration
steps.description:
For user access without sudo, add your user to the microk8s group:
```bash
sudo usermod -a -G microk8s <username>
```

steps.title: Verify Cluster Info
steps.description:
Ensure the cluster is operational by checking cluster information:
```bash
sudo microk8s kubectl cluster-info
```

uninstall.title: Remove MicroK8s
uninstall.description:
To uninstall MicroK8s, use the following command:
```bash
sudo snap remove microk8s
```

upgrade.title: Upgrade MicroK8s
upgrade.description:
To upgrade MicroK8s to a specific version, use the following command:
```bash
sudo snap refresh microk8s --channel=1.31/stable
```
Before upgrading, ensure to backup your configurations and data. You can check the current version with:
```bash
sudo microk8s version
```
If issues arise after the upgrade, you can rollback to the previous version using:
```bash
sudo snap revert microk8s
```

troubleshooting.title: MicroK8s not starting
troubleshooting.description:
If MicroK8s fails to start, check the logs for errors:
```bash
sudo journalctl -u snap.microk8s.daemon-kubelet
```
Ensure that your system meets the requirements and that Snap is installed correctly.

troubleshooting.title: kubectl command not found
troubleshooting.description:
If you receive a 'command not found' error for kubectl, ensure you have configured your kubeconfig correctly:
```bash
sudo microk8s kubectl config view --raw > $HOME/.kube/config
```

troubleshooting.title: Add-ons not working
troubleshooting.description:
If enabled add-ons are not functioning, check their status:
```bash
sudo microk8s status
```
Re-enable the add-on if necessary:
```bash
sudo microk8s disable <addon>
sudo microk8s enable <addon>
```

troubleshooting.title: Insufficient permissions
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter permission issues, ensure your user is added to the microk8s group:
```bash
sudo usermod -a -G microk8s <username>
```

versionNotes.changes: Improved performance and stability, added support for new Kubernetes features.
versionNotes.deprecations: Deprecated support for Kubernetes versions below 1.29.

versionNotes.changes: Introduced new add-ons and enhanced security features.
versionNotes.deprecations: Removed legacy add-ons that were not maintained.

resolution.summary: A successful setup of MicroK8s will have all nodes in a 'Ready' state, with the necessary add-ons enabled and accessible via kubectl. You should be able to deploy applications and manage your cluster seamlessly.
codeSnippets:
sudo microk8s status --wait-ready
sudo microk8s kubectl get nodes

sudo microk8s enable dns
sudo microk8s enable dashboard

prerequisites.description: Ensure Snap is installed on your Linux system.
